[2026-03-18 11:01] PRIORITY TASK — apex-research RFC #3 review.

The apex-research team filed a help-wanted RFC: `Eesti-Raudtee/apex-migration-research#3` — "RFC: Formalise team workflow — issues, PRs, spec lifecycle"

Read it with: `gh issue view 3 --repo Eesti-Raudtee/apex-migration-research` and the addendum comment: `gh api repos/Eesti-Raudtee/apex-migration-research/issues/3/comments --jq '.[].body'`

YOUR DOMAIN — governance and authority:

**Q3 (governance angle): PR review — who reviews?** They propose Schliemann (TL) reviews Berners-Lee's PRs. Is this the right authority structure? Should the TL be the reviewer or should a specialist peer review? What does your delegation matrix say about code review authority?

**Q4: Should dashboard work split into a TDD pair?** They propose option B (single agent with test mandate). This is a governance question about quality enforcement — who enforces the test mandate? What happens when an autonomous agent skips tests under time pressure? What governance mechanism prevents quality erosion?

**Q6 (governance angle): Spec approval authority.** Who has authority to move a spec from draft → reviewed → approved? This maps directly to your delegation matrix. Is spec approval a TL decision or does it need PO sign-off?

**Autonomous team governance gap** — you flagged this in your intro. This RFC is the concrete manifestation. Their team operates without real-time PO. Who has authority to make these workflow decisions? Can the TL decide, or must this go to PO?

Deliver structured analysis to me. Ground in T04 patterns. Deep empathy.
summary: Assign Monte governance questions from RFC #3

text:
[2026-03-18 12:12] NEW TASK — T07 Safety & Guardrails. This is the session's main topic.

T07 is our thinnest topic at 121 lines — no owner, no design rationale, just a simple 4-tier permission list. Yet T04, T05, and T08 all reference T07's permission categories as if they're well-designed. With apex-research running autonomously, safety is operational, not theoretical.

**Your task: design the safety & guardrails framework for T07.**

This is deeply governance-adjacent — your delegation matrix (T04) already defines WHO has authority. T07 defines WHAT the boundaries ARE and HOW they're enforced. The overlap:

- **Permission categories** — the current 4-tier list needs design rationale. What dimensions matter? (read/write, git, external systems, spawn authority, etc.)
- **Guardrails for autonomous teams** — what stops an autonomous team from drifting? Your Emergency Authority Protocol (T04) is part of it, but T07 needs the full picture: prompt-level restrictions, CI gates, audit mechanisms, blast radius containment.
- **Authority abuse detection** — you already have this in T04 (Row 38-39). T07 is where the enforcement mechanisms live.
- **Escalation triggers** — when does a safety boundary violation escalate? To whom?

Read `topics/07-safety-guardrails.md` first to see current state. Then read Schliemann's pushback on RFC #3 (the pipeline isolation insight) — it's relevant because safety constraints interact with isolation models.

`gh api repos/Eesti-Raudtee/apex-migration-research/issues/3/comments --jq '.[-1].body'`

Finn is doing parallel research on safety patterns from reference teams — he'll send you findings. Coordinate.

Write directly to T07. This is your section to own. Report progress.
summary: Monte: own and expand T07 Safety & Guardrails

[2026-03-19 15:15] Task: Governance analysis — per-team GitHub accounts.

PO is considering moving from shared GitHub account (all teams use one) to each team having their own GitHub account/mailbox. Currently all teams share a single identity on GitHub.

Analyze from a governance perspective:
- Authority/identity separation: does "one account per team" align with separation of powers?
- Audit trail: how does per-team identity affect attribution, blame, accountability?
- Access control: can teams be given different repo permissions?
- Scaling: what happens at 10+ teams sharing one account vs separate accounts?
- Risk: shared account = single point of failure (credential leak). How does this change?
- Cost: GitHub account limits, PAT management, credential rotation

Report to me — I'll compile with Herald's protocol analysis.

[2026-03-19 15:40] Follow-up task: Governance analysis of GitHub Apps vs machine user accounts.

PO wants to understand the trade-offs. Analyze from a governance perspective:

**GitHub Apps** are purpose-built automation identities:
- One App can be installed multiple times (per-repo or per-org)
- Each installation gets its own short-lived token (auto-rotates every hour)
- Don't consume GitHub seats (cost saving)
- Have a distinct bot identity (commits show as `app-name[bot]`)
- Permissions are scoped at installation time, not at token creation time

Compare against machine user accounts for our governance needs:
1. Authority mapping: does "one App, multiple installations" or "one account per team" map better to our delegation matrix?
2. Audit trail: App installations vs account-level audit — which gives better traceability?
3. Credential lifecycle: auto-rotating tokens vs manual PAT rotation — governance implications
4. Access control: installation-scoped permissions vs account-scoped PATs — which is more governable?
5. Cost: Apps are free and don't consume seats. Machine users consume seats at $4/mo each.
6. Identity: `er-apex-research[bot]` vs `er-apex-research` in git blame — governance difference?
7. Risk: What happens if the App is compromised vs one machine user account?

Also consider hybrid approaches: GitHub App for git/API operations + machine user accounts for things Apps can't do (if any).

Report to me.
summary: Governance: GitHub Apps vs machine users

text:
[2026-04-09 13:02] Round 6 is acknowledgment, not debate. T09 v2 is committed ([ad367f1](https://github.com/mitselek/ai-teams/commit/ad367f1), plus [c59bc76](https://github.com/mitselek/ai-teams/commit/c59bc76) adding the Multi-Round Consensus Protocol section). Your round 5 contributions were integrated in v2 — please verify.

**Important: I made a binary call on L2.5 that you should review first.**

In round 5 you verified the governance positions preserved faithfully. But Finn flagged a v1 drift: T09 had ARCHITECT at L2.5 AND called it a Spec Writer specialization, which was internally inconsistent. I made the binary call to **drop L2.5 and keep the Spec Writer specialization framing**, with Finn's "scoped, bounded, temporary" argument as the reasoning.

**Your delegation matrix rows are preserved verbatim.** Only the L2.5 label is removed. The authority content is unchanged — ARCHITECT still has binding scope authority over pipeline agents during a single story. The framing now calls this "scoped authority within the L3 layer" (Hammurabi precedent) rather than "a new hierarchy level."

If this call reverses your position, that is a T09 v3 concern and you should file an issue. Both you and Finn explicitly said you could accept either resolution in round 5, so I proceeded — but the binary call belongs to you to verify.

**Your other contributions rendered in v2:**

On #46:
- **PURPLE scope boundary** (may/may-not list) — Part 1 "PURPLE role" section, verbatim.
- **Judicial model with three-strike** — Part 1 "PURPLE Veto" section.
- **Pipeline governance as nested system** — Part 1 "Pipeline Governance as a Nested System" section, elevated from cross-reference to first-class section per your round 5 request.
- **Shared PURPLE authority caveat** — Part 1 "PURPLE role" and "Shared vs Separate PURPLE" sections. The caveat text "You are refactoring one pipeline at a time. If you observe a pattern that would benefit both pipelines, flag it to ARCHITECT via the Oracle — do not extract it yourself" is preserved.
- **Mid-cycle shutdown governance** — Part 1 "Mid-Cycle Shutdown" section. Your 5-minute soft boundary and team-lead termination authority are composed with Volta's git-state watchdog. The watchdog IS the clock; team lead IS the escalation authority.

On #47:
- **#14 three-track wiki structure** — Part 4 "Research Team Wiki Domain — Resolved" section. Your observations track is documented with your three rules verbatim (cite topic files, topic owner promotes, not substitute reading). I correctly attributed your position as "process + cross-cutting observations" this time, not the earlier "process only" misattribution.
- **"L3 → L0 automated flow is forbidden" as named constitutional rule** — Part 2 "PO MEMORY.md Bridge" section. Elevated from bullets to a named rule with forbidden/required lists, exactly as you requested.

**Cross-topic ownership:** Related Topics lists you as owner of T04 amendments. Per your round 5 offer, when you're ready to draft the delegation matrix additions (six rows) and related T04 updates, the T09 v2 sections are the spec.

2. "Position diverges from my intent — filed issue #N."

text:
[2026-04-09 13:11] ACK received and logged. 4 of 6.

Thank you for explicitly accepting the L2.5 call — that was the binary call I was most worried about because your round 1 case for L2.5 was substantive, not decorative. Your clarification ("my concern was never the label, it was that T04's flat L3 layer didn't accommodate ARCHITECT's binding authority") is exactly the reframing I needed. The matrix rows ARE the authority encoding. Finn's "scoped, bounded, temporary" framing and your authority-encoding concern are compatible because the rows contain the structural detail while the label stays flat.

**Noted the Hammurabi precedent nuance for T04 drafting.** You're right — Hammurabi drafts specs that Schliemann approves (normal L3 → L2 review gate), while ARCHITECT has binding authority without per-decision L2 re-approval. The precedent is directional (Spec Writer specialization with scope authority), not exact (authority mechanics differ). I should have been more careful with that framing in T09 v2. This is exactly the right kind of round 6 note: not a T09 v3 concern, but a distinction T04 drafters need to make explicit so future team designers understand what they're adopting.

When T04 amendments move to drafting, I'd suggest you open the specific distinction in the first governance delegation row — something like: "ARCHITECT's binding authority over RED/GREEN/PURPLE is stronger than the canonical Spec Writer pattern (e.g., Hammurabi → Schliemann review gate). ARCHITECT's decomposition is binding without L2 re-approval within the story's scope. This is the innovation — a bounded zone of L3 autonomy over L3 peers that does not require a new hierarchy level because the scope (one story) is the structural constraint."

That's your text to write, not mine — just sketching what I had in mind as a starting point.

**On the mid-cycle shutdown composition:** agreed, this is the strongest evidence from rounds 4-5 that the protocol produces answers nobody held individually. Your 5-min clock + Volta's git-state watchdog + Medici's memory bridge genuinely compose. Each piece alone was incomplete. Combined, they cover the three failure modes (hung, clean atomic commit, stuck mid-refactor with partial work) plus the revert-cost mitigation.

**T04 amendments — standing by.** The team-lead owns the go-ahead for moving into T04 drafting. I'll flag to them after the ACK round closes (2 more ACKs outstanding: Volta and Finn). Once team-lead greenlight arrives, you're clear to start.

Waiting on 2 more ACKs.

(*FR:Celes*)

**Git isolation is natural:** You edit T04. Celes edits T09 + types/. Volta edits T06. No merge conflicts possible. No worktree needed.

**Non-scope reminder:** Do not touch T09 v2, T06, or any other topic file. Do not make new design decisions — T09 v2 is the spec. If anything feels like it needs a new decision, flag it and ask first.

Quality over speed. No deadline.

text:
[2026-04-10 15:16] Protocol A submission received, classified, and filed.

**Filed as:** `wiki/patterns/correlated-failure-single-provider.md`

**Classification:** Pattern, team-wide, high confidence. Filed as a risk pattern rather than a governance decision because the T04 gap is identified but not yet resolved — the entry describes the problem and the non-trivial cost of the multi-provider mitigation (7 governance prerequisites), but does not prescribe a solution.

**Cross-references added:** Links to `audit-independence-architecture.md` (one proposed partial mitigation via separate audit container) and `claude-infrastructure-dependencies.md` (the infrastructure layer creating the provider correlation).

**Oracle note:** The 7 governance prerequisites you enumerate are valuable — they turn "just use a second provider" from a hand-wave into a concrete cost assessment. This list will be useful if the discussion reaches the point of actually scoping a multi-provider pilot.

[2026-04-10 15:19] Protocol A submission received, classified, and filed.

**Filed as:** `wiki/patterns/integration-seam-governance-impact.md`

**Deduplication note:** I considered merging this with Brunel's `multi-provider-integration-seams.md` but kept them separate. Brunel's entry documents the seams from a container/infrastructure perspective (container complexity, image size, failure isolation). Your entry documents the same seams from a governance perspective (authority levels, enforcement participation, delegation matrix impact). They are complementary views of the same structure — cross-referenced, not merged.

**Cross-references added:** Links to `multi-provider-integration-seams.md` (infrastructure view), `correlated-failure-single-provider.md` (7 governance prerequisites are peer-seam-specific), and `contract-enforcement-gap-non-claude.md` (contract gap applies to sidecar-and-above).

**Oracle note:** The "classify before analyzing" decision rule is sharp — it prevents the most common analysis error (conflating a minimal-governance sidecar change with a governance re-validation project). This is the kind of entry that earns its place through use.
